SIEM onboarding and assurance

LATCH turns log onboarding into a governed decision path.

LATCH helps assess whether a data source is worth onboarding, technically ready, operationally supportable and aligned to SIEM detection value. Use it to move from initial viability through logging pattern selection, assurance checks, data source catalogue alignment and operational governance.

What LATCH covers

LoggingSelect the right onboarding pattern, transport method, parsing path and ingestion architecture.
AssuranceScore viability, supportability, operational readiness, engineering complexity and implementation effort.
TaxonomyKeep data sources, patterns, owners, indexes, sourcetypes and controls consistently classified.
Checks HubReuse health checks for freshness, parsing, timestamp quality, volume, host coverage and platform resilience.

Start here

  1. Assess viabilityAnswer the calculator questions and review the calculated decision, blockers and radar.
  2. Review complexityConfirm transport, parsing, volume, analytics and engineering complexity before design.
  3. Select patternChoose the governed logging pattern and required firewall, auth and platform prerequisites.
  4. Confirm checksMap health checks for collection, freshness, parsing, timestamp quality and resilience.
  5. Catalogue sourceAlign the source family, owner, index, sourcetype, expected events and security use cases.
  6. Move to designProceed only when decision, ownership, supportability and implementation path are clear.

Quick actions

What LATCH produces

Viability decisionProceed, proceed with conditions, investigate or block.
Security valueWhether the data source has a clear detection or assurance purpose.
Technical readinessWhether collection, auth, routing, schema and samples are ready.
Operational assuranceWhether ownership, support, monitoring and handover are defined.
Supportability riskCustom-solution, documentation and reverse-engineering risk.
Engineering complexityIngestion architecture, parsing, normalisation and technical debt.
Implementation effortDifficulty, complexity and expected time to implement.
Next actionThe recommended action before onboarding or detailed design.
The default path is intentionally decision-first: assess value and viability before committing engineering time to collection, parsing, dashboards or detections.
70 taxonomy terms 721 current entries 16 populated sources 18 reference source families 39 governed logging patterns 390 reusable health checks Taxonomy explorer opens fully collapsed
Lifecycle & Dependency · TAX-06.04

Data Source Assurance

Operational assurance view centred on configured input components. It joins collection configuration, current test results, CIM compliance, detection coverage, dependencies and lifecycle evidence without duplicating the underlying taxonomy records. Select a source to review its end-to-end assurance profile or open the source directly in the taxonomy explorer.

Operational sources

Input components are the primary operational assurance record until a dedicated Data Sources register is populated.

0 visible
ComponentData sourceInput typePlatformOwnerHealthCIMDetectionAssurance

Onboarding pipeline

Lifecycle records remain separate from deployed components until a stable component or data-source identifier is assigned.

Lifecycle & Dependency · TAX-06.07

Log Onboarding Viability Assessment

Phase 1 decision gate used during the initial consultation. It separates security value from technical readiness, operational assurance and implementation effort, then applies supportability, custom-solution, reverse-engineering and discovery modifiers before a source proceeds to detailed design.

Interactive phase gate

Viability calculator

Enter consultation findings to calculate the viability score, question-based security value, question-based technical readiness, question-based operational assurance, supportability impact, reverse-engineering trigger, transport/ingestion complexity, data formatting/parsing complexity, log volume/EPS complexity, analytics/use-case mapping complexity, engineering complexity, implementation effort and recommended next action before detailed design.

Optional. Select the currently preferred pattern so it appears in the exported result.
Calculated from questions Answer the security value questions below. N/A answers are excluded.
Calculated from questions Answer the technical readiness questions below. N/A answers are excluded.
Calculated from questions Answer the operational assurance questions below. N/A answers are excluded.
Security value questions15 scored questions

These answers calculate the Security Value percentage. Use High/confirmed only when the source has clear business, detection, compliance or incident-response value; use Low/unknown when the value is assumed or not yet proven.

Technical readiness questions19 scored + complexity factors

These answers calculate the Technical Readiness percentage. Each question asks whether the source has enough evidence to move from viability into design/build. Use Confirmed/ready only when evidence is available; use Partial when a design assumption still needs validation.

Select Yes when the source cannot use a certified TA, supported API input, standard syslog/agent path, or other repeatable ingestion method.
Describe the likely build path, such as supported add-on/API, custom REST polling, custom parser, routing logic, or still to be confirmed.
Assess whether product, API, audit-log and integration documentation is easy to access, current, searchable and usable by the onboarding team.
Assess whether field dictionaries, API schemas, table layouts, object relationships, data types and constraints are documented and accessible.
Use Yes only when real or representative raw events are available for parsing, timestamp, field, CIM and detection validation.
Assess whether expected EPS, daily GB, peak bursts and retention/cost impact are known enough for sizing and approval.
Can the source reliably send, expose or export the required logs through a supported connector, API, syslog, database or file path?
Indicates whether onboarding can use a certified Splunk TA, a standard vendor export/API, a custom integration, or no known supported path.
Do we know where the events live, which event types are needed, and whether the collection account or collector can access them?
Confirms whether the required audit/security logs are enabled and whether any licence, plan, feature flag or subscription is required.
Checks whether required tenant IDs, subscriptions, endpoints, namespaces, database details, scopes, APIs or source-side settings are known.
Confirms the credential method, approval path and ownership for API keys, OAuth apps, service accounts, certificates or tokens.
Confirms ports, proxy, allowlists, private endpoints, routing and firewall/security-zone paths between the source, collectors and Splunk.
Checks TLS, certificate chain, mTLS, CA trust, certificate ownership and whether encryption meets platform/security requirements.
Describes how predictable the raw data is, such as JSON, CEF, key-value, syslog, CSV, multi-line or proprietary text.
Confirms whether event time is present, parseable, accurate, timezone-aware and not dependent on ingestion time.
Checks whether a TA, parser, sourcetype, props/transforms or field extraction approach already exists or must be created.
Confirms the fields needed for detection, CIM, entity mapping and investigation, such as user, src, dest, action, status and object.
Confirms whether fields can map to Splunk CIM or the detection schema needed by existing correlation searches and dashboards.
Assesses whether expected storage, licence, index, retention, filtering and infrastructure impact are known and accepted.
Confirms the target architecture, such as direct to Splunk Cloud/on-prem, heavy forwarder, syslog server, ingest processor or security-zone intermediary.

Technical complexity factors. These answers sit under Technical readiness but remain separate from the Technical Readiness percentage. They continue to calculate transport, parsing, volume/EPS, analytics, engineering complexity and overall implementation effort.

Transport & ingestion method complexity1 complexity input

This answer calculates the transport part of implementation complexity. Low = native/cloud connector or standard vendor API. Medium = standard syslog or a lightweight pre-configured agent. High = custom REST/script/webhook, database polling/JDBC, or air-gapped collection.

Calculated from transport method Select the transport and ingestion method.
Data formatting & parsing complexity1 complexity input

This answer calculates the parsing part of implementation complexity. Low = natively supported vendor formats or structured JSON/CEF where a parser exists. Medium = semi-structured logs or custom apps needing parser modifications. High = unstructured raw text, multi-line logs or proprietary formats requiring complex regex from scratch.

Calculated from data format Select the data formatting and parsing method.
Log volume & EPS complexity1 complexity input

This answer calculates the size part of implementation complexity. Low = low EPS and predictable volume. Medium = moderate or fluctuating volume needing basic load balancing or edge filtering. High = massive, noisy streams such as firewall traffic, DNS query logs or endpoint EDR telemetry that need dedicated forwarders, heavy filtering and storage planning.

Calculated from volume and EPS profile Select the expected log volume and EPS profile.
Analytics / use case & value mapping complexity1 complexity input

This answer calculates the analytics part of implementation complexity. Low = compliance-only storage or out-of-the-box vendor detection rules. Medium = mapping custom fields to a standard schema such as Splunk CIM, Elastic ECS or Microsoft Sentinel ASIM. High = brand-new multi-source correlation rules or behavior/anomaly analytics.

Calculated from analytics mapping profile Select the expected use-case and analytics mapping profile.
Engineering complexitycalculated from existing answers

Engineering complexity is calculated from existing viability answers across ingestion architecture, parsing & normalisation, and maintenance & technical debt. It uses a 1–4 matrix: 1 = low, 2 = medium, 3 = high, 4 = very high. The equivalent implementation rating is also considered in the combined complexity calculation.

Calculated from existing answers Uses transport method, integration path, routing and network/firewall answers.
Calculated from existing answers Uses parser availability, data format, fields and CIM/use-case mapping answers.
Calculated from existing answers Uses custom solution, supportability, documentation, runbook, monitoring and change-notification answers.
Calculated from engineering matrix The engineering score uses the highest score across ingestion, parsing and maintenance debt.
Combined implementation complexitycalculated summary

Implementation complexity uses the highest rating across transport/ingestion, data formatting/parsing, log volume/EPS, analytics/use-case mapping, engagement complexity and the engineering-complexity equivalent rating. Engagement complexity uses an evidence score out of 18: higher scores mean lower engagement complexity.

Calculated from answered questions Difficulty is derived from readiness blockers, discovery gaps, supportability, sample evidence and operational maturity.
Calculated from engagement evidence score Uses documentation, schema, log location, integration support, parser readiness and custom/reverse-engineering pressure.
Calculated from transport, parsing, volume/EPS, analytics, engagement and engineering The implementation complexity uses the highest rating across transport/ingestion, data formatting/parsing, log volume/EPS, analytics/use-case mapping, engagement complexity and engineering complexity.
Score rangeRatingEngagement complexity
15–18Low ComplexityLogs are well documented, locations and schemas are known, and integration is strongly supported.
10–14Medium ComplexitySome documentation or support exists, but validation, parsing, or custom work is required.
5–9High ComplexityLimited documentation, unclear schemas, or weak integration support.
0–4Very High ComplexitySignificant discovery, reverse engineering, vendor engagement, or custom development required.
Operational assurance questions15 scored questions

These answers calculate the Operational Assurance percentage. Use Confirmed/ready only when ownership, support and operating evidence are known; use Partial when a support assumption still needs validation.

Phase 1 viability records

Use these records to hold or progress onboarding before schema, fields, collection mechanisms and support responsibilities are fully defined.

Data Collection & Routing · TAX-03.01

SIEM Data Source Catalogue

Reference catalogue of the security and operational source families the SIEM should cover. Catalogue records describe intended telemetry and use-case value; explicit mappings connect them to configured input components, current assurance results and onboarding work without treating reference requirements as deployed inputs.

Reference source families

Coverage is derived from approved stable-ID mappings to operational components, onboarding records and registered platforms.

0 visible
SourceCategorySub-categoryPriorityCoverageImplementationsOnboardingAssurance

Logging Patterns Table

Compare all 39 governed logging patterns, including conditional port/firewall, authentication and source-platform prerequisites. Ports shown are common defaults and must be validated against the approved local design.

Collection questions

Governance & Assurance · TAX-02.09

Naming Convention

A single standard for taxonomy registry IDs, Splunk object names, event-health checks, detection content and analytical assets.

Technical-name pattern

Use this structure where a technical object needs platform, dataset, purpose and environment context.

<domain>_ <platform>_ <component>_ <purpose>_ <environment>
Examplesec_azure_entra_signin_prodValid structure
Grandfathering: existing production indexes and sourcetypes do not need to be renamed solely for style. Record exceptions and apply the standard to new or materially changed objects.

Name validator

Check a proposed name against the selected governed pattern.

Pattern catalogue

Registry identifiers align with the current taxonomy and workbook records; technical patterns define how new Splunk content should be named.

ObjectPatternExampleSeparator / caseScope
Platform IDPLT-###PLT-003Hyphen / uppercaseRegistry
Source Family IDSRC-###SRC-001Hyphen / uppercaseReference catalogue
Governance Profile IDGOV-###GOV-003Hyphen / uppercaseRegistry
Logging Pattern IDLP-###LP-006Hyphen / uppercaseRegistry
Input Component IDIC-###IC-011Hyphen / uppercaseRegistry
Collector IDCOL-###COL-005Hyphen / uppercaseRegistry
App / TA IDAPP-###APP-003Hyphen / uppercaseRegistry
Stanza IDSTZ-###STZ-021Hyphen / uppercaseRegistry
Health Test IDTC-<TYPE>-###TC-HEC-004Hyphen / uppercaseAssurance
Dependency IDDEP-###DEP-007Hyphen / uppercaseLineage
Onboarding IDONB-###ONB-005Hyphen / uppercaseWorkflow
Viability Assessment IDVIA-###VIA-001Hyphen / uppercaseWorkflow / phase gate
Index<domain_or_platform>[_<dataset>]aws_cloudtrailUnderscore / lowercaseSplunk
Sourcetype<vendor>:<product>:<dataset>azure:aad:signinColon / lowercase preferredSplunk
Event Health CheckHC_<platform>_<source>_<check>HC_entra_signin_freshnessUnderscore / lowercaseAssurance
Detection Use Case IDDET-<DOMAIN>-###DET-IDM-012Hyphen / uppercaseDetection
Detection Search<Domain> - <Platform> - <Behaviour>Identity - Entra ID - Repeated MFA DenialTitle case / spaced hyphenDetection
Dashboard<Domain> - <Platform> - <Audience>Security - Entra ID - OperationsTitle case / spaced hyphenAnalytics

Governance rules

01Stable identifiers

Never reuse an ID after retirement. Display names can change; registry IDs must remain stable.

02Controlled separators

Use hyphens for registry IDs, underscores for technical names and colons for sourcetype hierarchy.

03No embedded secrets

Never include tokens, credentials, personal data or sensitive business values in object names.

04Environment last

When environment is required, use a controlled suffix such as dev, test, uat or prod.

Use

  • TC-AWS-002
  • HC_entra_signin_freshness
  • azure:aad:signin
  • Identity - Entra ID - Repeated MFA Denial

Avoid

  • new_test_2
  • AzureLogs
  • hec-token-prod-9f2…
  • Critical Alert
